title: Band 8 Collocations
description: High-register academic collocations that lift IELTS Writing and Speaking answers from band 6-7 phrasing to band 8+ — the fixed word partnerships examiners expect around problems, causes, policy, and argument.

cards.definition: to represent a danger or risk to something; used when describing how serious a problem is.
cards.example: Rising sea levels pose a threat to low-lying coastal cities.

cards.definition: to carry out a systematic study or investigation into a topic.
cards.example: Scientists conducted research into the long-term effects of screen time on children.

cards.definition: generally agreed to be true by most people; useful for stating a common view before supporting or challenging it.
cards.example: It is widely accepted that early childhood education improves later academic performance.

cards.definition: steadily increasing pressure or demand on someone to act.
cards.example: Governments are under mounting pressure to reduce carbon emissions.

cards.definition: to deal with or attempt to solve a problem, often used when proposing solutions.
cards.example: Local authorities must address the issue of traffic congestion before it worsens.

cards.definition: to form a judgement based on evidence; common when summarising an argument.
cards.example: From this data, one can draw the conclusion that diet has a direct impact on longevity.

cards.definition: to be extremely important in achieving or influencing something.
cards.example: Renewable energy will play a vital role in the transition away from fossil fuels.

cards.definition: to cause something to happen or exist, typically a problem or an effect.
cards.example: The rapid growth of cities has given rise to serious housing shortages.

cards.definition: to make an existing problem worse; useful when criticising a policy or trend.
cards.example: Cutting public transport funding would only exacerbate the problem of urban pollution.

cards.definition: to control or limit an increase in something, usually something undesirable.
cards.example: New regulations aim to curb the growth of plastic waste.

cards.definition: to act in an extreme or forceful way because a situation is serious.
cards.example: Authorities were forced to take drastic measures to contain the outbreak.

cards.definition: to find a satisfactory compromise between two competing demands.
cards.example: Parents must strike a balance between discipline and freedom when raising teenagers.

cards.definition: to make more people aware of an issue, typically through a campaign.
cards.example: The charity's campaign helped raise awareness of the dangers of plastic pollution.

cards.definition: to officially introduce rules that limit an activity.
cards.example: The city imposed restrictions on the use of private vehicles in the centre.

cards.definition: to produce important or noteworthy outcomes, often from research or a policy.
cards.example: The new teaching method yielded significant results within a single term.

cards.definition: to gradually gather strength or support; used for trends or movements.
cards.example: The movement for a four-day working week has gained momentum in recent years.

cards.definition: to reduce the difference between two things, such as income levels or generations.
cards.example: Government subsidies are intended to bridge the gap between rich and poor regions.

cards.definition: to receive the positive results of an action, often after time or effort.
cards.example: Employees who work remotely often reap the benefits of a better work-life balance.

cards.definition: to reduce the harmful impact of something.
cards.example: Coastal barriers can help mitigate the effects of rising sea levels.

cards.definition: to make a determined effort to deal with a difficult problem.
cards.example: The report recommends tackling the issue of youth unemployment through vocational training.

cards.definition: to suffer the main negative impact of something, more than others.
cards.example: Small businesses often bear the brunt of sudden changes in tax policy.

cards.definition: to have a strong influence on a decision or opinion.
cards.example: Peer opinion carries considerable weight when teenagers make lifestyle choices.

cards.definition: to cause a sudden and often heated public disagreement.
cards.example: The proposal to build a new airport sparked controversy among local residents.

cards.definition: to experience a significant and often positive change.
cards.example: The city centre has undergone a transformation over the past decade.

cards.definition: to use power or authority to affect a situation or person.
cards.example: Multinational corporations exert considerable influence over global trade policy.

cards.definition: to be criticised by many people or groups.
cards.example: The government's decision attracted widespread criticism from environmental groups.

cards.definition: to establish an example or rule that others are likely to follow.
cards.example: The court ruling set a precedent for future cases involving digital privacy.

cards.definition: to cause a series of connected events, often negative.
cards.example: A single factory closure can trigger a chain reaction of job losses in a small town.

cards.definition: to officially decide to follow a particular course of action.
cards.example: Several countries have adopted a policy of taxing sugary drinks.

cards.definition: to put a plan into action.
cards.example: The company implemented a strategy to reduce its carbon footprint by 2030.

cards.definition: to distribute money, time, or materials for a particular purpose.
cards.example: Hospitals must allocate resources carefully during a public health crisis.

cards.definition: to produce income, typically for a government or business.
cards.example: Tourism generates significant revenue for many coastal economies.

cards.definition: to encourage the development of new ideas or methods.
cards.example: Universities play a key role in fostering innovation through research funding.

cards.definition: to deliberately develop a regular pattern of behaviour.
cards.example: Schools can help students cultivate the habit of reading for pleasure.

cards.definition: to gradually weaken people's trust in something.
cards.example: Repeated scandals have undermined public confidence in the banking sector.

cards.definition: to make a difficult situation even more complicated or serious.
cards.example: A shortage of housing compounds the problem of urban poverty.

cards.definition: to make the suffering caused by poverty less severe.
cards.example: Microfinance schemes have helped alleviate poverty in rural communities.

cards.definition: to use up a region's raw materials faster than they can be replaced.
cards.example: Overfishing continues to deplete natural resources in coastal waters.

cards.definition: to cause harm or destruction, often used with environmental or physical impact.
cards.example: Industrial pollution inflicts lasting damage on river ecosystems.

cards.definition: to become liable for expenses as a result of an action.
cards.example: Delaying maintenance often means a company incurs higher costs later.

cards.definition: to keep an economy growing steadily over time.
cards.example: Investment in education is essential to sustain economic growth.

cards.definition: to encourage economic activity, often through government spending.
cards.example: Tax cuts are sometimes used to stimulate the economy during a downturn.

cards.definition: to slow down or obstruct improvement or development.
cards.example: Bureaucratic delays continue to hamper progress on the housing project.

cards.definition: to make it easier for people to exchange information.
cards.example: Modern technology has done much to facilitate communication across borders.

cards.definition: to improve the rate at which work is completed.
cards.example: Flexible working hours have been shown to enhance productivity.

cards.definition: to make a procedure simpler and more efficient.
cards.example: The new software streamlines the process of applying for a visa.

cards.definition: to make something work as effectively as possible with minimal waste.
cards.example: Factories are redesigning production lines to maximise efficiency.

cards.definition: to reduce the likelihood or severity of a negative outcome.
cards.example: Diversifying investments is a common way to minimise risk.

cards.definition: to treat safety as more important than other considerations.
cards.example: Construction firms must prioritise safety over speed of completion.

cards.definition: to protect the natural world from harm.
cards.example: Stricter laws are needed to safeguard the environment from industrial waste.

cards.definition: to protect the variety of plant and animal life in an area.
cards.example: National parks play a crucial role in preserving biodiversity.

cards.definition: to take action against the causes or effects of climate change.
cards.example: International cooperation is essential to combat climate change effectively.

cards.definition: to reduce the amount of harmful gases released into the atmosphere.
cards.example: The policy aims to curb emissions from the transport sector.

cards.definition: to encourage practices that avoid depleting resources for the future.
cards.example: Many companies now promote sustainability as part of their brand image.

cards.definition: to make people notice or focus on something.
cards.example: The documentary draws attention to the plight of endangered species.

cards.definition: to provide new information that helps explain something unclear.
cards.example: The study sheds light on why some students underperform despite ability.

cards.definition: to cause doubt about whether something is true or valid.
cards.example: The scandal called into question the integrity of the entire electoral process.

cards.definition: to be examined closely and critically, often by the public or authorities.
cards.example: The company's environmental record has come under increasing scrutiny.

cards.definition: to start to become popular or widely accepted.
cards.example: The idea of a universal basic income is gaining traction among policymakers.

cards.definition: to have value or be worth serious consideration.
cards.example: The proposal holds merit, even if it would be costly to implement.

cards.definition: to justify additional study because something is uncertain or significant.
cards.example: The unusual pattern in the results warrants further investigation.

cards.definition: to arrive at general agreement among a group with differing views.
cards.example: It took several rounds of talks before the committee reached a consensus.

cards.definition: to openly express worries or objections.
cards.example: Residents voiced concerns about the noise from the new construction site.

cards.definition: to carefully consider the advantages and disadvantages of something.
cards.example: Before relocating abroad, it is worth weighing the pros and cons carefully.

cards.definition: to be greater or more significant than the advantages of something.
cards.example: Critics argue that the risks of the project outweigh the benefits.

cards.definition: proof or information persuasive enough to convince someone.
cards.example: There is compelling evidence that early intervention reduces long-term costs.

cards.definition: a very large proportion of a group, used to describe strong general agreement.
cards.example: An overwhelming majority of respondents supported the new policy.

cards.definition: the fundamental or root reason behind a problem, as opposed to its symptoms.
cards.example: Poverty is often the underlying cause of poor educational outcomes.

cards.definition: effects that extend widely and affect many areas or people.
cards.example: The policy change is likely to have far-reaching consequences for small farmers.

cards.definition: a solution that is practical and likely to work.
cards.example: Researchers are searching for a viable solution to the region's water shortage.
